text: The surgeon completes the lymph node dissection, carefully excising lymphatic tissue from the surrounding area.
The surgeon searches for the pubic bone as the first landmark, proceeding to open the lateral side of the umbilical artery.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens as a landmark and proceeds to dissect it, ensuring it is taken down smoothly without complications.
The surgeon completes the process of dropping the bladder, indicating that the bladder is fully mobilized and separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully approaches the endopelvic fascia, preparing to incise it to better delineate the border of the prostate. The bladder is emptied again to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon clears the bladder, which appears visibly full, creating more space in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the periprosthetic fat surrounding the prostate and prepares it for submission to the final pathology.
The surgeon carefully isolates the dorsal vein, avoiding distal movement to prevent retraction and potential bleeding.
The surgeon reaches the border of the bladder, revealing a small prostate that appears reduced in size, resembling post-transurethral resection (TUR) changes.
The surgeon exposes the periprostatic fat, which is visible surrounding the prostate gland.
The surgeon applies traction to identify the best plane for incision, making a minor cut to confirm the correct entry point into the surgical field.
The surgeon gently pushes the muscle fibers aside to reveal the shape and contours of the prostate, providing a clearer view of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon gently pushes muscle fibers away to assess the shape and distal end of the small prostate, navigating the challenging anatomy due to its reduced size.

The surgeon applies traction and uses a Maryland dissector to spread tissue, aiming to access deeper areas without nerve sparing on this side. The arterial part is identified as a region of concern for cancer presence.
The surgeon makes an incision through the endopelvic fascia, revealing the levator muscle fibers. The cancerous area is identified as being larger on the left side.
The surgeon prepares to open the bladder, using traction to reveal the base of the prostate. The incision indicates the entry point into the bladder, aiding in identifying the bladder neck location.
The surgeon applies significant traction, revealing the detrusor muscle fibers, which guide the surgical approach.
The surgeon deflates the balloon and opens the bladder, then carefully manipulates the catheter by pulling it back slightly and repositioning it.
The surgeon observes the bladder neck, noting its appearance and condition during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes the bladder neck and proceeds to follow the contour of the prostate, indicating the direction of dissection.
The surgeon inspects the bladder wall, ensuring it is not made too thin during the procedure. The camera view shows the interior of the bladder, with the orifices visible at a distance.

The surgeon uses the ProGrasp instrument to pull the catheter slightly, revealing the detrusor fibers of the bladder wall. The fibers become visible as traction is applied, and the surgeon carefully manipulates the catheter to avoid buttonholing.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the prostate, ensuring not to inadvertently enter it, while double-checking the thickness of the bladder wall to confirm it is adequate.

The surgeon removes urine from the bladder, which is visibly distended. The bladder is gently pushed to adjust its position, and the surgeon grasps the border of the bladder neck to proceed with the dissection.
The surgeon identifies a bleeding vessel and uses suction to manage the bleeding while applying counter traction with a Maryland dissector. Visible fibers are observed in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection, moving further down until the vas deferens is visible. The vas is opened slightly during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies and isolates one of the vas deferens, using it to lift the structure. Two hemolog clips are placed on the bladder neck to open the space further.

The surgeon places the first hemolog clip on the perivesical fat near the prostate, ensuring it is not too deep to avoid entering the prostate. An open vessel is visible, and the clip is positioned carefully. The same procedure is repeated on the left side, with further isolation of the area.
The surgeon identifies the seminal vesicle and cuts around it, noting the absence of vessels in the area. The seminal vesicle is further exposed for better visualization.
The surgeon opens the space further, revealing the left vas deferens. The suction device is positioned on the vas deferens to manage any potential bleeding.
The surgeon follows the vas deferens further down, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for bleeding while preparing to clip the seminal vesicle, using traditional techniques to secure the structure.
The surgeon places a hemolog clip on a larger vessel, ensuring it is positioned away from the bladder neck for safety.

The surgeon follows the vas deferens on the opposite side, revealing the denovus fascia covering the seminal vesicle. The fascia is pushed towards the midline to identify the plane, and the fascia is cut to further isolate the seminal vesicle. A vessel is identified and prepared for ligation with a hemolog clip.
The surgeon carefully follows the vas deferens downwards, applying small clips to larger vessels for safety. The seminal vesicle is then isolated for further manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the Denonvilliers fascia and begins to peel it off from the seminal vesicle, ensuring the correct anatomical plane is maintained for better visualization of the vesicle.

The surgeon checks if the seminal vesicle has been adequately freed, pushing the surrounding tissue further down to facilitate nerve-sparing on the left side. The prosthetic pillar is visible during this maneuver.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect underneath the prostate, utilizing small clips for precision. The assistant provides crucial support in maintaining optimal exposure and traction, with careful attention to anatomical structures and lighting.

The surgeon manipulates the prostate to identify the plane, focusing on the inner waist fascia within the periprostatic fat. Using scissors, they perform a blunt dissection on the right side, lifting the prostate slightly to reveal the intrafascial layer.
The surgeon uses a zero-degree camera angle throughout the procedure, applying small clips to secure tissue as they work underneath the prostate.

The surgeon identifies an important anatomical plane for orientation during the prostatectomy, attempting to follow it despite challenges. There is visible oozing in the surgical field, and a small blue clip is applied to control the bleeding.
The surgeon identifies the high anterior anatomy and proceeds to release the prostate at its base, preparing the area for clip placement.

The surgeon identifies the lateral fascia covering the nerves, carefully pushing it aside to avoid the nerve structures. A clear window is visible, though the area appears sticky, prompting a change in approach to address the fibers above. Small vessels are clearly identified during this process.
The surgeon identifies the capsule of the prostate and attempts to push the surrounding fibers away, following the plane from a posterior approach. The dissection is developed from the rectal side.
The surgical team approaches the apex of the prostate, a critical and visually distinct part of the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon visualizes the apex of the prostate and gently pushes the neurovascular bundles away to expose the capsule. The prostate is pulled slightly to improve visibility. A surgical clip is placed near the apex, with the urethra visible starting at this point.

The surgeon examines the midline area, ensuring the prostate is treated on the rectal side. Scissors are turned and gently opened to create a larger surface area, carefully pushing the tissue without rupturing it.

The surgeon begins a non-nerve sparing procedure on one side, using hemolog clips to secure tissue. The surgical plane is identified, and cautery is reintroduced to dissect through the visible fat tissue.
The surgeon uses an extra-facial approach to dissect through fatty tissue, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon identifies the denovus fascia and places an additional hemolog clip in the area, ensuring proper alignment and securing tissue.
The surgeon identifies one of the arteries previously discussed, visible within the surgical field, while carefully manipulating tissue around the prostate fossa.
The surgeon navigates close to the prostate, avoiding straying too far. The surrounding fat tissue is visible, and the camera lens is cleaned before approaching the apex of the prostate.

The surgeon inserts the catheter slightly to visualize its tip, highlighting the position of fibers around the prostate. The Maryland forceps are used to grasp the prostate, with visible vessels nearby that may be addressed.
The surgeon carefully develops the apex of the prostate, entering the centaurine fascia and centaurine plexus. The focus is on avoiding bleeding while ensuring clear visualization of the apex.
The surgeon identifies the border between the sphincter muscle and the apex of the prostate, focusing on visualizing the apex during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the correct plane for dissection near the urethra, moving slightly away from the apex. Muscle fibers appear whitish, marking the border between the muscle and the prostate. The surgeon decides to remove the tissue at the border of the prostate.
The surgeon navigates down to the catheter, encountering some arterial bleeding. The bleeding is minimal and manageable. The Santorini's plexus is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon frees the tissue around the neurovascular bundle using cautery, as preservation is not necessary in this area. The prostate is then taken, revealing the rectolateral aspect and identifying the muscle shape. Pure fat is visible with no nerves present.

The surgeon cuts through the urethra and seminal vesicle area, where nerves were previously attached, focusing on the apical region and the basis. The inner side of the dissected tissue appears yellow.
The surgeon begins suturing the dorsal vein complex using Vicryl sutures, with visible instruments positioned in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a suture in the distal urethra and locks it in place, demonstrating the technique used for securing the suture.
The surgeon prepares to perform the Rocco suture, a modified technique, noting minimal bleeding in the surgical field.

The surgeon adapts the bladder using two layers of Rocco suture. The first layer moves the bladder down, while the second layer involves grabbing the mucosa. The posterior layer is secured with a substantial bite. The ileal conduit is mobilized, and the right urethral dissection is performed, prepared with a handmade sling.
The surgeon manipulates the denoviae fascia, aiming to position it underneath the urethra with a firm grasp using a Maryland dissector. Bilateral stents are placed in the ilial conduct.

The surgeon identifies the border of the Denovese fascia and prepares to move it downward. A clip may be used to secure the fascia, although it can slide back due to the non-absorbable suture. The fascia is manipulated carefully to avoid rupture.
The surgeon applies a clip to facilitate the procedure, securing the border of the Denovese fascia. The surgical field is made smaller, and minor oozing is stopped.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any bleeding, identifying a small vessel that requires clipping. A small clip is applied to control the bleeding, ensuring the area is secure before completing the Rocco suture.
The surgeon manipulates the bladder neck, which has a small opening, aiming to grasp the edge of the mucosa for precise positioning.
The surgeon lifts the catheter slightly to expose the retracted mucosa within the narrow pelvic space, adjusting the catheter position to avoid collision with the pelvis and gain more working space.

The surgeon observes minor oozing in the surgical field and adjusts the catheter slightly to improve access. The mucosa edge is visible, and care is taken not to close the space excessively during the procedure.
The surgeon holds the thread with one arm to prevent it from sliding back, ensuring that the mucosa edges are touching during the suturing process.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to tie a knot, ensuring good resistance in the midline of the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully sutures the complex, deliberately avoiding incorporation of the sphincter muscle into the anastomosis, ensuring a strong suture line while sparing the functional tissue.
The surgeon observes the mucosa and the bladder neck, avoiding vascular structures and the sutured complex on this side. The procedure will be repeated on the anterior side.

The surgeon grasps the sutured dorsal vein complex while performing the procedure. The robotic approach provides easier access to the neurovascular bundle, facilitating the completion of the anastomosis. A catheter is placed as part of the final steps.
The surgeon cuts the sutures after placing the Rocco suture, ensuring the catheter is properly positioned and blocked. The bladder is then filled with the first syringe of fluid.

The surgeon observes a leakage on the lateral side of the bladder neck after filling it with an additional 200 milliliters. Small bubbles are visible, indicating the location of the leakage. The bladder is emptied to create space for lymph node dissection.
The surgeon places a suture to approximate the tissue closer to the thread, adjusting the lengths of the suture ends by cutting one short and leaving the other longer.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel and prepares to address it before proceeding to the lymph node dissection. Scissors are positioned on robotic arm three, ready for use in the dissection process.
The surgeon follows the anatomical structure down to the ureter, opening the tissue further to expose the area. Two Hem-o-lok clips are requested for use in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the urethra, gonadal vein, and thickened lymphatic tissue in the surgical field, with the epigastric vessels visible above.
Four minutes later, methylene blue dye is observed leaking into the retroperitoneal space, indicating a breach in the tissue integrity.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and proceeds to open the surrounding tissue further, revealing limited anatomical structures in the area.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and cautions against grabbing it, while noting the presence of the internal iliac artery nearby.
The surgeon gently pushes the ureter aside to gain better access to the surgical area.
The surgical field is exposed, revealing lymph nodes that can be taken. The genital femoral nerve is identified as a landmark, with the surgeon avoiding lateral dissection beyond this point.
The surgeon identifies an enlarged lymph node within the surgical field, exercising caution during manipulation.
The surgeon moves down to the obturator fossa, requesting the bladder to be emptied to improve visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any signs of bleeding, focusing on the side wall and upper area. Attention is then directed towards locating the artery.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum to expose the vas deferens and identifies the nearby nerve and the edge of the vein.
The surgeon manipulates tissue to free it further, identifying the ureter in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, noting the minimal surrounding tissue, and proceeds to manipulate the area using surgical instruments.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery and prepares to take it using surgical instruments.
The surgeon identifies a vessel and maintains its position, ensuring it remains visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates around a visible nerve while observing the entry point of a vein. Tissue is being removed in the area, demonstrating progress in the procedure.
